getting_to_know_your_child_and_family.childs_current_or_previous_early_childho:
Elena has primarily been in a home-based care setting with a gradual introduction to more structured group environments. From birth until about 18 months, she was at home full-time with me (Isabel) while I was on an extended maternity leave and then working part-time from home. During that period, her day-to-day care was shared between me, my husband Daniel, and occasionally her maternal grandmother, who lives about 15 minutes away in Minneapolis.

Around 18 months, when I returned to work full-time, we hired a nanny who came to our home four days a week from 8:00am to 4:30pm. Our nanny, Maria, has a background in early childhood education and incorporated a lot of Montessori-inspired activities into Elena’s daily routine—things like low shelves with accessible toys, child-sized kitchen tools for helping prepare snacks, and plenty of time for independent play and exploration. Maria would also take Elena to the neighborhood park most mornings, where she interacted with a small, consistent group of children in the 18 months to 3 years age range.

At age 3, we enrolled Elena in a small, play-based preschool program two mornings a week (Tuesdays and Thursdays from 9:00am–12:00pm) at a local community center in St. Paul. The preschool focused on socialization, basic routines (circle time, snack, outdoor play), and early exposure to group learning. Elena adapted well to that setting—she initially preferred to observe quietly from the edges of group activities but, over the course of a few months, became more engaged, especially during art projects and story time. She developed a strong bond with one of her teachers, Ms. Karen, and often talked about her classmates at home.

Currently, Elena still attends that same preschool two mornings a week, and on the other weekdays she is either at home with our nanny or with one of us if we are working from home. We have tried to keep her daily rhythm predictable: breakfast together as a family, some form of outdoor time in the morning (walks around the block, backyard play, or the park), quiet time after lunch (often looking at books by herself or listening to audiobooks), and free play in the afternoon. She is used to cleaning up her materials before moving on to something else, and she often imitates “school” at home by lining up her stuffed animals and pretending to be their teacher.

In addition, we have been intentionally supporting her independence in self-care tasks. She dresses herself (with some help for tricky buttons), uses the toilet independently, washes hands thoroughly, and helps set the table and clear her dishes. We feel that her previous experiences—home-based care with a nanny who values independence, plus part-time preschool—have helped her feel comfortable separating from us and following simple routines with other adults and children.
getting_to_know_your_child_and_family.reservations_or_concerns_about_having_yo:
Our main reservations and concerns are less about Elena’s ability to be in a school setting and more about ensuring that the environment is a good fit for her temperament and developmental pace.

Elena tends to be a thoughtful, somewhat cautious child in new situations. She doesn’t usually jump right into new groups or activities; she prefers to observe quietly for a while, take everything in, and then join when she feels ready. In her current preschool, this meant that for the first few weeks she watched circle time from my lap or from the back of the room before participating more fully. Our concern is that in a larger or more fast-paced classroom, her quiet, observant nature might be misinterpreted as disinterest or shyness, when in fact she is just processing and needs a bit of time before actively engaging.

We also want to make sure that transitions are handled gently. Elena does fine with separating from us now, but it took some practice. What helped was having a predictable goodbye routine (two hugs, a kiss on the hand, and waving at the window) and teachers who understood that she might need a few extra minutes of reassurance in the beginning. We would feel more at ease knowing that her teachers at Cathedral Hill Montessori are comfortable supporting children who need a gradual warm-up period in the morning rather than expecting instant separation.

Another area we think about is sensory sensitivity. Elena is generally easygoing, but she can become overwhelmed in very noisy or chaotic environments. For example, large indoor birthday parties at play centers have sometimes led to her covering her ears and wanting to leave earlier than other children. She does much better in calm, orderly settings where she can focus on one activity at a time. This is actually one of the reasons we’re drawn to Montessori, but our concern is that if the classroom ever becomes too loud or unstructured, she might withdraw rather than speak up for herself.

We also wonder about how conflict between children is handled. Elena is not aggressive and tends to back away if another child grabs a toy or speaks harshly. While we know that learning to navigate conflict is a normal part of social development, we hope she will be supported in finding her voice—learning to say things like, “I was using that; can I have it back when you’re done?” instead of quietly giving up and feeling upset inside. Our concern is that without gentle guidance from adults, she could become the child who is always yielding to others.

Finally, as parents, we are always balancing our desire to encourage independence with wanting to make sure she still feels emotionally connected and secure. Moving from a smaller, part-time preschool to a full-day Montessori program is a significant change. We want to be sure that communication between home and school is strong so that if there are any signs that she is struggling—changes in mood, sleep, appetite, or behavior—her teachers will let us know and we can work together to support her. Overall, we are excited about Elena being in a school setting; our concerns are mostly about making the transition as smooth as possible and ensuring that her quiet strengths are recognized and nurtured.
